As machine learning becomes increasingly widespread across different sectors, it encounters key challenges such as data privacy, uneven data distribution, and limited data access. Federated learning (FL) emerges as a decentralized and privacy-preserving solution that enables model training across distributed devices without sharing raw data. This study presents a comparative analysis of two optimization algorithms, FedAvg and FedProx, within the FL framework. The focus is placed on evaluating their behavior under non-IID settings, simulated through different usage contexts: IoT (1 class/client), mobile (2 classes/client), and desktop (5 classes/client). Using the MNIST dataset and the LeNet model, the evaluation investigates metrics such as accuracy and convergence over 200 communication rounds. The findings aim to highlight how each algorithm handles data heterogeneity and to guide future FL deployments in real-world scenarios.

 Federated Learning Optimization Algorithms Comparative Analysis Introduction

The swift growth of machine learning across various application areas has led to an increased need for privacy-preserving and decentralized methods, marking the emergence of FL. In contrast to traditional centralized training, FL facilitates collaborative model development among distributed devices without the need to share raw data. However, this model presents a significant challenge: data heterogeneity. Users and devices supply data collected under very diverse conditions, ranging from IoT sensors with limited patterns to mobile applications with moderate variations, to desktop systems with more diverse usage patterns. This type of variability results in non-independent and non-identically distributed (non-IID ${ }^{1}$) data, which significantly complicates optimization and convergence processes in FL.

Despite the growing body of work on federated optimization, most studies either assume simplified data partitions or focus on a single experimental setting. Consequently, the relative strengths and weaknesses of popular algorithms such as FedAvg and FedProx remain insufficiently understood when confronted with realistic non-IID data. Specifically, there has been a lack of focus on how various application contexts (IoT,

In the context of federated learning, this means that different clients (devices) may have datasets with varying distributions, leading to difficulties in learning and model convergence. [15]

mobile, desktop) affect heterogeneity and subsequently impact the performance of algorithms. This paper addresses this gap by providing a comparative analysis of FedAvg and FedProx under varying context-aware partitions of the MNIST dataset, highlighting how each algorithm adapts to heterogeneous data distributions.

The remainder of this paper is organized as follows. Section II provides background on FL concepts and foundations. Section III reviews related work on the use of FL in non-IID contexts and on federated optimization algorithms. Section IV presents the experimental setup, including dataset partitioning to simulate different contexts, the model architecture, and evaluation metrics. Section $\mathbf{V}$ presents and examines the findings from the comparative analysis across various contextaware scenarios. Finally, Section VI concludes the paper and suggests avenues for future research.

 Background Overview of Federated Learning

FL is a method invented by Google in 2016 [2], defining this innovation as a decentralized approach to model training, which is a specific case of distributed training, while focusing on data confidentiality. The main aim of this method is to provide effective training to the global model through updates developed by clients (which refers to the devices that participate in the training).

The field of FL has gained great popularity in recent years, and various applications of this algorithm have been realized, starting with the first application developed by Google [3], which exploited FL to train, evaluate and deploy a model to improve the quality of search suggestions on the Gboard virtual keyboard, without direct access to user data. Subsequently, FL applications have become extremely common and were outlined in the review [4], including Privacy-Sensitive Healthcare, recommender systems, Smart Cities and other industries. The key issue in all these areas is the need to preserve data confidentiality, or the lack of centralized data.

The accompanying illustration Fig. 1 shows the typical architecture of FL, or more precisely, the process of model

Illustration of the federated learning architecture

formation using the FL method. What differentiates this type of learning from others is its ability to preserve data confidentiality while enabling collaborative learning. This illustration shows the key elements that contribute to model building:

Server: the central component that shares the global model, gets model updates from clients and handles the aggregation of these updates to create a high-performance model after a number of training iterations.

Clients: a key aspect of the configuration, which can be constituted by mobile devices, computers, IoT devices, enterprise systems and so on. Their main function is to train the model provided by the server on their respective local data.

To provide a more comprehensive understanding of the architectural models of federated learning, we refer to the study by [5], which offer a collection of architectural patterns to address the design challenges of FL systems.

Foundations for Fl Algorithm Development

In this section, we describe the main steps involved in building a FL algorithm.

Data partitioning: Data partitioning in FL is a way of implementing the decentralized aspect of this approach. It primarily relates to how data is allocated among clients involved in model development and training, when distributing a substantial dataset among them. This concept differs from the case where each client has its own data, which differs from each other. The manner in which data is partitioned in a FL model varies depending on the nature of the data, the aim of the model, the number of clients and the technical constraints of the clients and the system. In this article [7], the authors have experimented with their system using the three main data partitioning methods (horizontal partitioning, vertical partitioning and hybrid partitioning), and have tested the performance of each approach on the training of the model.

Client Selection: Since FL operates on distributed devices whose availability varies, it is important to select an optimal fraction of clients for each training round. Involving all available clients can lead to inefficiencies due to communication constraints. The challenge is to find a balance between model convergence, computational efficiency and fairness while taking into account factors such as data heterogeneity, device availability as well as network conditions. This study [9] provides a representative overview of existing challenges and opportunities in FL client selection, covering the most general client selection criteria that are widely applicable to FL systems. In addition, it presents a comprehensive review of existing FL client selection algorithms. Among these algorithms, FedCS (Federated Client Selection) [8] is a protocol aimed at enhancing client selection in federated learning by considering the computational resources and network conditions of clients. This algorithm provides several benefits compared to other client selection techniques, especially regarding increased efficiency and adaptability. This algorithm aims to select as many clients as possible within a specified deadline. Additionally, there are other well-known techniques, such as random selection. It is a simple, fair, and scalable approach to FL. However, it does have its limitations when it comes to addressing client heterogeneity and ensuring efficient training.

The selection of clients for model training is not necessarily done on real devices. This is known as the Virtual Client Engine, a method of simulating multiple clients on a single device. This approach is particularly adopted FL simulation frameworks such as Flower, TensorFlow Federated (TFF), PySyft and FedML. It offers a number of advantages, including a reduction in the need for physical devices.

Server-Client Interaction and Model Aggregation: FL is acknowledged for its ability to develop machine learning models in a decentralized and collaborative manner. This methodology is built on two essential components: The server, which acts the role of orchestrator, coordinating the learning process, and the clients, who train the model locally using their own data. The interaction between these two entities is a key step in the process. The aim of this communication is to enable the server to aggregate the updates sent by the clients to produce an improved global model. This paper [10] explores different aggregation strategies, focusing on their role in updating global models. Furthermore, it examines the benefits and drawbacks of each approach, emphasizing the specific updates shared by the clients, which differ from one strategy to another (e.g., model parameter updates, gradient updates, weight updates, etc.).

Related Work

Like most decentralized systems, FL inherently faces challenges related to heterogeneity, which arise from several factors: statistical heterogeneity (differences in data distribution between clients), system heterogeneity (variations in computing power, memory and connectivity), which often leads to stragglers ${ }^{2}$, and device heterogeneity (different device types and reliability), and behavioral heterogeneity (differences in participation or data generation patterns). In addition, model-heterogeneous FL has been the subject of numerous studies, such as [17], which focus specifically on model heterogeneity but also discuss its interaction with other heterogeneity types. Meanwhile, other works explore the interplay between data heterogeneity and system heterogeneity [18]. Surveys such as [16] emphasize that the combination of these factors degrades the overall performance of the global model, particularly when data are non-IID.

To mitigate the impact of heterogeneity in FL, several methodologies have been proposed in recent years.

Among the promising methods to tackle heterogeneity in FL, optimization approaches have been the subject of numerous studies. These approaches adapt learning to the non-IID nature of data distributions, unbalanced participation, and variability at the system level. Table I lists a variety of the more popular optimization algorithms in the field of FL, with their defining characteristics and use cases adapted to each algorithm.

In parallel with optimization-based solutions, various clientlevel and data-level methods have been proposed to address heterogeneity in FL. Among them, Client selection strategies aim to accelerate convergence and improve model quality by focusing on participants that provide the most informative or reliable updates. Wolfrath et al. [19] proposed a cluster-based client selection approach that accounts for heterogeneity by considering variations at both the system and statistical levels, thereby mitigating the effect of stragglers and reducing the proportion of imbalanced data. In another direction, clusteringbased approaches have been developed to handle highly dispersed data distributions. Long et al. [20] introduced MultiCenter Federated Learning, which clusters clients and trains multiple global models in order to approximate the global model or converge local subsets toward a consistent model. More recently, methods that take data quality into account have been introduced. They dynamically adjust client participation based on data quality measures, so that noisy or low-quality local data does not overly affect the learned global model.

However, personalization-based techniques for dealing with heterogeneity in FL have also been proposed. For instance, Deng et al. [21] introduced TailorFL, a dual-personalized framework that adapts both the model structure and its parameters to each client's computing power and data distribution. Through a combination of a resource-aware pruning strategy and a scale-based aggregation mechanism, TailorFL enables devices with varying computing capabilities and nonIID data distributions to train effectively while maintaining personalized models for local inference. Experimental results demonstrate that TailorFL not only improves accuracy in heterogeneous conditions, but also reduces communication

Stragglers are clients (devices) that require much more time to train their local model and communicate with the server.

and computational costs. Personalization thus appears to be a promising solution to be explored.

A range of approaches-including optimization, client selection, clustering, data quality management, and personal-ization-have been proposed to reduce heterogeneity in FL. Other research has used context-aware FL to consider the conditions that lead to heterogeneity and adjust training processes accordingly. For example, Huang et al. [22] demonstrate how context-awareness (system and network conditions) can directly mitigate heterogeneity in FL by estimating each client's training and reporting phases before they participate. This method mitigates the adverse impacts of stragglers and enhances training effectiveness in diverse mobile settings. Qu et al. [23] show Context-Aware Online Client Selection (COCS) for hierarchical FL, which leverages contextual data such as computational capacity and connection quality to make dynamic client selections. Their findings demonstrate that integrating context into the client selection process can significantly enhance convergence speed, fairness, and model accuracy in heterogeneous environments.

Previous research emphasizes the variety of approaches to reduce heterogeneity in FL; however, the specific influence of application context has not been fully examined. In this regard, our study adds to this growing field by assessing federated optimization algorithms in IoT, mobile, and desktop environments.

 Experimental Setup

To analyze the effect of device context in the FL approach on model heterogeneity, we simulated three architectures, each representing a different context (IoT, mobile, desktop). These three settings cover a wide range in terms of data diversity. For instance, IoT devices typically focus on a specific function, resulting in homogeneous data; however, when these data are shared among multiple clients, they become highly heterogeneous. In contrast, the mobile and desktop contexts represent environments with higher data diversity. Thus, once the data is partitioned among clients, the level of heterogeneity between devices decreases compared to the IoT case. To this end, we conducted a series of simulations using both the FedAvg and FedProx algorithms to compare their performance across different scenarios.

To simulate our context-based study, we used the MNIST dataset, a widely recognized benchmark for image classification. We selected a representative sample of the data for our experiments and distributed it across 10 clients in a nonIID manner. Each client received data corresponding to its assigned context, in order to simulate realistic usage scenarios (see Table II).

To train our model, we used the LeNet architecture because of its compatibility with the MNIST dataset and its demonstrated effectiveness in digit classification tasks. The server orchestrates the training process by collecting model updates from clients and aggregating them into a global model during each communication round. This process ensures data confidentiality, as the server cannot access local client data. To Comparison of federated learning optimization algorithms

FedProx [11] FedProx is an extension of the FedAvg algorithm that incorporates a proximal term into the local loss function, penalizing deviations of the local model from the global model. Scenarios with heterogeneous data and computational resources.
FedOpt (FedAdam, FedYogi, FedAdagrad) This algorithm extends the standard FedAvg algorithm by integrating adaptive optimization techniques such as Adam, AdaGrad, and Yogi into the global model update process. Models that require faster convergence.
FedNova [12] FedNova is a federated learning approach designed to address objective inconsistency in heterogeneous settings by normalizing local updates to ensure fair and stable global aggregation. Systems with a variable number of local optimization steps and differences in local objectives.

optimize training, we focused on two widely used federated algorithms - FedAvg and FedProx - with the aim of evaluating their performance in different usage contexts. FedAvg is the core algorithm that averages local client updates to learn a global model. On the other hand, FedProx modifies FedAvg by adding a proximity term $\mu$ in the local objective that ensures local updates remain close to the global model. This variation makes FedProx more robust to heterogeneity, as it reduces divergence caused by highly non-IID data distributions.

The majority of hyperparameters remained constant in each simulation, with only two elements changing: the data context, which was modified by changing the number of local classes per client to represent IoT, mobile, or desktop environments; and the specific FL algorithm used (FedAvg or FedProx). In all cases, the training was conducted over 200 communication rounds using 10 clients, each performing 3 local epochs per round.

 Results and Discussion

The results of all simulations were illustrated in two graphs: one for the FedAvg algorithm and the other for the FedProx algorithm. Both Figs. 2 and 3 show the evolution of model accuracy across the three usage contexts: desktop, mobile, and IoT. We place particular emphasis on the IoT context, as it illustrates the most critical non-IID scenario due to extreme class imbalance (1 class per client).

From Fig. 2, the findings indicate strong performance in a desktop environment, where the model quickly converges and reaches an accuracy of approximately 0.95. In comparison, within a mobile framework, FedAvg exhibits a slower convergence speed, with accuracy leveling off at about 0.80 after 200 communication rounds. The IoT setting is marked by significantly unbalanced data, leading to the lowest performance metrics: accuracy gradually improves but shows considerable fluctuations, ultimately stabilizing at around 0.50. This highlights FedAvg's vulnerability to extreme variations in data.

In Fig. 3, FedProx results are shown under the same conditions. In the desktop setting, it achieves the highest accuracy, approaching 0.95. In the mobile environment, FedProx shows similar performance to FedAvg, achieving around 0.80, with slightly more stable convergence. In the context of IoT, FedProx shows significant improvements compared to FedAvg, with its final accuracy increasing from approximately 0.50 to 0.58. This suggests that the proximity component in FedProx successfully reduces the divergence caused by nonIID data, thereby improving its robustness in highly diverse environments.

Fedavg accuracy over 200 rounds under different usage contexts

Fedprox accuracy over 200 rounds under different usage contexts

Based on the results illustrated in the two figures, it is evident that the context of the devices participating in FL training affects the convergence of the model, due to the way data are distributed across clients. However, the comparison of the two optimization algorithms used shows how each of them works to handle heterogeneity under these setups. As expected, FedProx demonstrates a significant improvement in the most critical case, namely the IoT setting.

 Conclusion

This study investigated the performance of two prominent federated learning algorithms, FedAvg and FedProx, in a context-sensitive simulation using the MNIST dataset. By simulating non-IID conditions among clients, we assessed the ability of each algorithm to handle data heterogeneity. The results revealed that context-aware data distribution significantly affects model performance, with FedProx showing better stability in highly heterogeneous environments.

Although this study focused on a simulated environment using the MNIST dataset, future work could delve into context-aware federated learning using real-world datasets to better reflect practical applications. In addition, although many studies have compared federated learning algorithms, there is still a need for further research in this area.

In addition, model-heterogeneous FL has been the subject of numerous studies, such as [17], which focus specifically on model heterogeneity but also discuss its interaction with other heterogeneity types. Meanwhile, other works explore the interplay between data heterogeneity and system heterogeneity [18]. Surveys such as [16] emphasize that the combination of these factors degrades the overall performance of the global model, particularly when data are non-IID.

To mitigate the impact of heterogeneity in FL, several methodologies have been proposed in recent years.

Among the promising methods to tackle heterogeneity in FL, optimization approaches have been the subject of numerous studies. These approaches adapt learning to the non-IID nature of data distributions, unbalanced participation, and variability at the system level. Table I lists a variety of the more popular optimization algorithms in the field of FL, with their defining characteristics and use cases adapted to each algorithm.

In parallel with optimization-based solutions, various clientlevel and data-level methods have been proposed to address heterogeneity in FL. Among them, Client selection strategies aim to accelerate convergence and improve model quality by focusing on participants that provide the most informative or reliable updates. Wolfrath et al. [19] proposed a cluster-based client selection approach that accounts for heterogeneity by considering variations at both the system and statistical levels, thereby mitigating the effect of stragglers and reducing the proportion of imbalanced data. In another direction, clusteringbased approaches have been developed to handle highly dispersed data distributions. Long et al. [20] introduced Multi-Center Federated Learning, which clusters clients and trains multiple global models in order to approximate the global model or converge local subsets toward a consistent model. More recently, methods that take data quality into account have been introduced. They dynamically adjust client participation based on data quality measures, so that noisy or low-quality local data does not overly affect the learned global model.

However, personalization-based techniques for dealing with heterogeneity in FL have also been proposed. For instance, Deng et al. [21] introduced TailorFL, a dual-personalized framework that adapts both the model structure and its parameters to each client's computing power and data distribution. Through a combination of a resource-aware pruning strategy and a scale-based aggregation mechanism, TailorFL enables devices with varying computing capabilities and non-IID data distributions to train effectively while maintaining personalized models for local inference. Experimental results demonstrate that TailorFL not only improves accuracy in heterogeneous conditions, but also reduces communication and computational costs. Personalization thus appears to be a promising solution to be explored.

A range of approaches-including optimization, client selection, clustering, data quality management, and personal-ization-have been proposed to reduce heterogeneity in FL. Other research has used context-aware FL to consider the conditions that lead to heterogeneity and adjust training processes accordingly. For example, Huang et al. [22] demonstrate how context-awareness (system and network conditions) can directly mitigate heterogeneity in FL by estimating each client's training and reporting phases before they participate. This method mitigates the adverse impacts of stragglers and enhances training effectiveness in diverse mobile settings. Qu et al. [23] show Context-Aware Online Client Selection (COCS) for hierarchical FL, which leverages contextual data such as computational capacity and connection quality to make dynamic client selections. Their findings demonstrate that integrating context into the client selection process can significantly enhance convergence speed, fairness, and model accuracy in heterogeneous environments.

Previous research emphasizes the variety of approaches to reduce heterogeneity in FL; however, the specific influence of application context has not been fully examined. In this regard, our study adds to this growing field by assessing federated optimization algorithms in IoT, mobile, and desktop environments.

 Experimental Setup

To analyze the effect of device context in the FL approach on model heterogeneity, we simulated three architectures, each representing a different context (IoT, mobile, desktop). These three settings cover a wide range in terms of data diversity. For instance, IoT devices typically focus on a specific function, resulting in homogeneous data; however, when these data are shared among multiple clients, they become highly heterogeneous. In contrast, the mobile and desktop contexts represent environments with higher data diversity. Thus, once the data is partitioned among clients, the level of heterogeneity between devices decreases compared to the IoT case. To this end, we conducted a series of simulations using both the FedAvg and FedProx algorithms to compare their performance across different scenarios.

To simulate our context-based study, we used the MNIST dataset, a widely recognized benchmark for image classification. We selected a representative sample of the data for our experiments and distributed it across 10 clients in a nonIID manner. Each client received data corresponding to its assigned context, in order to simulate realistic usage scenarios (see Table II).

To train our model, we used the LeNet architecture because of its compatibility with the MNIST dataset and its demonstrated effectiveness in digit classification tasks. The server orchestrates the training process by collecting model updates from clients and aggregating them into a global model during each communication round. This process ensures data confidentiality, as the server cannot access local client data. To optimize training, we focused on two widely used federated algorithms - FedAvg and FedProx - with the aim of evaluating their performance in different usage contexts. FedAvg is the core algorithm that averages local client updates to learn a global model. On the other hand, FedProx modifies FedAvg by adding a proximity term $\mu$ in the local objective that ensures local updates remain close to the global model. This variation makes FedProx more robust to heterogeneity, as it reduces divergence caused by highly non-IID data distributions. Comparison of federated learning optimization algorithms

The majority of hyperparameters remained constant in each simulation, with only two elements changing: the data context, which was modified by changing the number of local classes per client to represent IoT, mobile, or desktop environments; and the specific FL algorithm used (FedAvg or FedProx). In all cases, the training was conducted over 200 communication rounds using 10 clients, each performing 3 local epochs per round.

 Results and Discussion

The results of all simulations were illustrated in two graphs: one for the FedAvg algorithm and the other for the FedProx algorithm. Both Figs. 2 and 3 show the evolution of model accuracy across the three usage contexts: desktop, mobile, and IoT. We place particular emphasis on the IoT context, as it illustrates the most critical non-IID scenario due to extreme class imbalance (1 class per client).

From Fig. 2, the findings indicate strong performance in a desktop environment, where the model quickly converges and reaches an accuracy of approximately 0.95. In comparison, within a mobile framework, FedAvg exhibits a slower convergence speed, with accuracy leveling off at about 0.80 after 200 communication rounds. The IoT setting is marked by significantly unbalanced data, leading to the lowest performance metrics: accuracy gradually improves but shows considerable fluctuations, ultimately stabilizing at around 0.50. This highlights FedAvg's vulnerability to extreme variations in data.

In Fig. 3, FedProx results are shown under the same conditions. In the desktop setting, it achieves the highest accuracy, approaching 0.95. In the mobile environment, FedProx shows similar performance to FedAvg, achieving around 0.80, with slightly more stable convergence. In the context of IoT, FedProx shows significant improvements compared to FedAvg, with its final accuracy increasing from approximately 0.50 to 0.58. This suggests that the proximity component in FedProx successfully reduces the divergence caused by nonIID data, thereby improving its robustness in highly diverse environments.

Based on the results illustrated in the two figures, it is evident that the context of the devices participating in FL training affects the convergence of the model, due to the way data are distributed across clients. However, the comparison of the two optimization algorithms used shows how each of them works to handle heterogeneity under these setups. As expected, FedProx demonstrates a significant improvement in the most critical case, namely the IoT setting.

 Conclusion

This study investigated the performance of two prominent federated learning algorithms, FedAvg and FedProx, in a context-sensitive simulation using the MNIST dataset. By simulating non-IID conditions among clients, we assessed the ability of each algorithm to handle data heterogeneity. The results revealed that context-aware data distribution significantly affects model performance, with FedProx showing better stability in highly heterogeneous environments.

Although this study focused on a simulated environment using the MNIST dataset, future work could delve into context-aware federated learning using real-world datasets to better reflect practical applications. In addition, although many studies have compared federated learning algorithms, there is still a need for further research in this area.

Face liveness detection is essential for ensuring authenticity in electronic Know Your Customer (eKYC) and face recognition systems, particularly to prevent unauthorized access via presentation attacks (PAs). Passive methods, which require no user interaction, offer user-friendly solutions but often struggle to generalize across diverse PA types and environmental conditions. To address this challenge, this paper proposes a theoretical modular passive liveness detection framework based on a Region-Specific Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architecture, where each expert specializes in detecting specific liveness cues. The proposed framework is designed to include three experts, each focused on the eye region, skin texture, and environmental interaction. As a first step, this work presents the preliminary implementation and evaluation of the Skin Liveness Expert. The uniform LBP texture descriptors are extracted from skin regions and classified using various classifiers. On the CASIA-FASD dataset (in-domain), the best model Fully Connected Neural Network (FCNN) acieves an accuracy of 97.20 %, in cross-dataset evaluation (CASIA $\rightarrow$ NUAA), it achieves an accuracy of 94.20 %, indicating that region-specific texture-based cues transfer well. While the Eye Expert, Environmental Expert, and adaptive fusion mechanism remain under development, this paper establishes the foundation of a modular MoE framework with the potential to improve cross-dataset generalization and robustness against diverse attacks such as photos, replay videos, 3D masks, and deepfakes.

 Passive face liveness detection Mixture-ofExperts presentation attack detection cross-dataset generalization biometric security \DeclareUnicodeCharacter{2192}{\ifmmode\rightarrow\else{$\rightarrow$}\fi} Introduction

The need for strong security frameworks has increased as biometric technologies, particularly facial recognition, are becoming integrated in sensitive areas like financial transactions, access control, and personal device security [1]. These systems are excellent at recognizing and authenticating people using their facial characteristics, but they are becoming more susceptible to spoofing or Presentation Attacks (PAs), in which attackers take advantage of easily accessible or fraudulent biometric data to obtain unauthorized access [2], [3]. These vulnerabilities pose significant risks, including financial fraud, identity theft, and breaches of privacy. Security measures such as Presentation Attack Detection (PAD) systems, which aim to identify and stop spoofing attacks, are vital in reducing these threats [4], [5]. Though face recognition systems are very good at identifying people based on facial features, they can't tell the difference between a real, live person (a genuine user) and a spoofing/presentation attacker (an imposter). PAD systems tackle this problem [6], [7]. Attackers frequently mimic authentic users by displaying a spoof face, a fake duplicate of a real user's face made using printed pictures, replay videos, 2D, 3D masks, or cosmetically manipulated faces, or digitally manipulated images (e.g., deepfakes) [4]. The inability to differentiate between live and fake faces emphasizes the significance of face liveness detection as a critical security element in current face recognition systems, particularly those employed for access control and authentication [1].

A face liveness detection system is one of the PAD techniques, attempting to solve this issue by spotting PAs and guaranteeing that only live faces can access protected systems [1], [8], [9]. These algorithms can efficiently classify spoofing attempts when PAs are detected in conditions similar to those on which the system was trained [10]. However, such systems perform much worse in unconstrained conditions, new attack types, or cross-database settings, where environmental characteristics like illumination, camera quality, and human variety change from training circumstances [11], [12]. This restriction exposes a fundamental flaw in the reliability and scalability of current face liveness detection algorithms.

Existing face, or in general, liveness detection techniques are divided into two categories: active and passive [13]. Active methods need the user to do specified actions, such as blinking or nodding, to indicate their existence [14]. While successful, these solutions degrade the user experience and are not suitable for real-time or hands-free applications [13]. In contrast, passive methods may detect liveness from small facial and environmental cues alone, without requiring any interaction from the user [15]. While passive solutions are user-friendly and easy to implement, they often struggle in dynamic or unstructured environments [16]. They are especially vulnerable to sophisticated spoofing methods, such as AI-generated deepfakes and high-resolution 3D masks, which may provide very realistic and misleading biometric representations [17], [18]. As fraudsters use advanced technology, the fabricated identities they create become more realistic and complex, presenting substantial difficulties to the security and integrity of biometric systems. Traditional detection approaches, which were not intended to handle these developing threats, frequently fail, compromising the security and integrity of face recognition systems [8].

Despite significant advancements in passive face liveness detection technologies, current solutions face persistent challenges in balancing accuracy, real-time processing, and generalization, especially in the face of increasingly sophisticated PAs. These limitations have particular significance in uncontrolled environments, where factors such as lighting, camera quality, and subject diversity vary significantly from training conditions. Monolithic models often fail to generalize across such variability, leading to decreased robustness and reliability.

This research aims to address these challenges by proposing a theoretical and conceptual modular passive face liveness detection framework based on a region-specific Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architecture, which specializes different facial regions and cues to particular experts to improve generalization. The primary aim of this research is to develop an optimised passive facial liveness detection system that effectively distinguishes live faces from spoofed ones, including complex attacks, like 3D masks and deepfakes, while maintaining computational efficiency.

The paper is structured into five sections. Section I introduces the research, detailing the context, aims, and objectives. Section II offers an examination of related research work in passive face liveness detection research. Section III describes the proposed approach, specifying the framework and design. Section IV presents the preliminary experimental results from the initial module implementation. Lastly, Section V concludes the paper with a summary of findings and future work.

 Related Work

This section discusses related research on passive facial liveness detection methods, emphasizing advancements from recent years. It examines studies that focus on PAD mechanisms to secure biometric systems against Facial PA, particularly through passive facial liveness detection techniques that do not require user interaction. The section summarizes research papers on passive facial liveness detection methods (non-AI-based and AI-based) and the dataset.

Passive Face Liveness Detection Techniques

Passive face liveness detection (PAD) aims to distinguish between live and spoofed faces without requiring user interaction. Traditional methods relied on handcrafted features like Local Binary Patterns (LBP) [10], [19], [20], motion cues [17], or frequency analysis and Fourier transforms [20], [21]. These techniques were often combined with threshold-based [10], [17], [20]–[23] or classical machine learning classifiers like SVMs [24]–[26], logistic regression [27], and other approaches [19].

With the rise of deep learning, CNN-based models became popular due to their ability to learn discriminative features directly from raw images [16], [18], [28]–[32]. Hybrid models combining CNNs and LSTMs [33], Hybrid models with other deep and machine learning models, ResNet, and SVM [34] or transfer learning [35], or lightweight architectures [36]–[38] such as LivenessNet [15], MobileNetV2 [39] have demonstrated improved accuracy.

However, these models often suffer from limited generalizability across datasets due to their monolithic design, which fails to adapt to varying spoofing conditions such as lighting, device quality, or attack type.

 Mixture-of-Experts (Moe) Approaches

While most face liveness or PAs detection systems adopt monolithic models, a few studies have explored modular or expert-based strategies to improve generalization and robustness against diverse PAs conditions

One of the earliest works is by Kollreider et al. [40], who proposed a liveness verification system that integrates multiple handcrafted experts. Their system combines 3D motion cues and eye-blinking detection using optical flow and velocity analysis, all without relying on specialized hardware. Though pioneering for its time, this approach is non-AI and relies on rigid rule-based thresholds, limiting adaptability to more sophisticated and unseen attacks.

In recent years, there has been a shift toward AI-based Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architectures. Notably, Zhou et al. [41] introduced an Adaptive Mixture of Experts Learning (AMEL) framework, where each expert is trained on a specific dataset (e.g., CASIA, MSU) to capture domain-specific spoofing patterns. A Dynamic Expert Aggregation (DEA) module combines these expert outputs based on relevance to the test sample. Furthermore, a meta-learning strategy simulates unseen domains, enabling strong generalization across datasets. While AMEL achieves excellent performance (e.g., 11.31 % HTER on unseen OULU), it assumes access to clearly separable domain-specific training data, and its relevance scoring may degrade if domain similarity is misestimated. Moreover, the study focuses only on physical PAs types, it doesn't consider digitally face-manipulated attack types.

Another study by Chen et al. [42] proposed MoAE-CR (Mixture-of-Attack-Experts with Class Regularization) for unified detection of both physical and digital face attacks. This model employs soft expert routing and class regularization modules to handle intra-class variance (e.g., various spoof types) and inter-class similarity (e.g., live vs. highly realistic attacks). Despite achieving state-of-the-art results on complex datasets like UniAttackData and JFSFDB, this architecture is computationally heavy and lacks analysis of real-time inference. A different perspective is offered by a study [43] on face forgery detection (digital face manipulations, not strictly liveness detection) that used Adaptive Learning From Experts (ALFE). Each expert is trained on a specific forgery type (e.g., Deepfakes, FaceSwap), and a student model learns to distill knowledge adaptively based on prediction confidence. Although effective for forgery classification, this work is taskspecific and does not address live vs. spoof discrimination, which is central to PAD. Another study focused on digital PAs [44], the authors introduced the Deepfake Mixture of Experts (DFMoE) model, a Heterogeneous Mixture of Experts (HMoE) model. Their architecture employs a dynamic gating network to select and combine expert subnetworks of varying capacities and scales based on input characteristics. By coupling expert specialization with adaptive data augmentation, DFMoE enhances robustness against both known and unseen forgery types. Related direction utilizes feature fusion with specialized Vision Transformer (ViTs) encoders [45]. The authors employ three distinct ViTs, DaViT, iFormer, and GPViT, each focusing on capturing global context, frequency domain artifacts, and highresolution details, respectively. These are merged via an MLP-Mixer fusion module, achieving state-of-the-art deepfake detection performance across FaceForensics ++ and Celeb-DF benchmarks. While not using expert routing, this method demonstrates the power of modality-specialized feature processing in forgery detection.

Unlike previous works, as summarized in Table I, our proposed model introduces a conceptual modular framework, a region-specific MoE framework for passive face liveness detection. Instead of specializing experts by domain or attack type, which often limits flexibility when encountering new or mixed spoofing methods, we design experts that each focus on a specific visual cue, such as eye region, skin texture, or environmental consistency. This allows our model to capture diverse liveness indicators in parallel, enhancing generalization across unseen attacks, and reducing dependence on curated datasets.

 Methodology and System Design

This study proposes a novel conceptual modular, passive face liveness detection system using a region-specific MoE architecture. The architecture decomposes the detection task into three parallel expert modules, where the design of each expert module specializes in a specific visual or behavioral core module: Eye Liveness expert, Skin Liveness expert, and Environmental expert. This region-driven modularization approach was chosen to improve the system's ability to generalize across diverse face PA scenarios and environments. By isolating distinct tasks into specialized modules, the system can focus on specific abnormal features present in different spoofing methods, such as the lack of natural blinking in video attacks or unrealistic skin textures in maskbased attacks. This enables targeted detection strategies that increase the overall robustness of the system.

Architecture Overview

As illustrated in Figure 1, the system comprises three core components:

Expert Modules: Each expert is designed as a lightweight neural subnetwork that learns specialized features from its corresponding Region of Interest (ROI).

Overview of the proposed region-specific mixture-of-experts (moe) framework for passive face liveness detection.

\setcounter{enumi}{1}

Gating Network: A high-level context-aware gating module would dynamically modulate the contribution weights of each expert's output based on input characteristics.

Fusion & Classifier: Expert outputs would be fused through a soft attention mechanism or gated summation, producing a final liveness score.

Expert Modules and Learning Objectives

Each expert learns to encode region-specific liveness cues by optimizing task-driven objectives over localized input features.

The Eye Expert learns temporal motion patterns using 3D convolutional blocks or stacked LSTMs/CNNs. Supervisory signals are driven by blink occurrence, gaze deviation, and inter-frame motion entropy. This module is particularly sensitive to replay attacks and static image spoofs.

The Skin Expert focuses on high-frequency texture descriptors (e.g., pores, specular reflections). It is trained using patch-level classification losses, texture regularization, and spatial smoothness constraints. This module focuses on detecting presentation artifacts in printed images and silicone masks.

The Environmental Expert analyzes global consistency between the facial foreground and the environmental background. Features include shadow coherence, background motion, and illumination dynamics. It uses regression and contrastive losses to model natural scene cues and detect compositional inconsistencies often present in digital or recombined inputs.

Stepwise development process for proposed region-specific mixture-of-experts (moe) architecture for passive face liveness detection.

All experts operate independently and may share weights in initial convolutional layers to reduce model size.

 Adaptive Fusion Mechanism

Unlike traditional MoE models that perform hard routing, we adopt a soft routing mechanism via a gating network. The gating module receives a global context vector (e.g., pooled features from the shared encoder or a separate attention summary) and outputs a soft weight vector $w=[w 1, w 2, w 3]$ via a softmax layer. These weights determine the contribution of each expert: \begin{equation*} F_{\text {fused }}=\sum_{i=1}^{3} w_{i} \cdot F_{i} \tag{1} \end{equation*} where $F_{i}$ denotes the feature vector from the $i^{\text {th }}$ expert, and $w_{i}$ is its corresponding weight produced by the gating network. The gating module will be jointly trained with the experts using a combined loss function defined in Equation~(\ref{eqn2}). \begin{equation*} L_{\text {total }}=L_{\text {cls }}+\lambda \cdot L_{\text {gate-reg }}+\mu \cdot L_{\text {expert-indep }} \tag{1} \end{equation*}

$L_{\text {total }}$: binary cross-entropy loss for final liveness classification.

$L_{\text {gate-reg: }}$: optional entropy regularization to encourage confident gating decisions.

$L_{\text {expert-indep }}$: optional penalty to encourage diversity in expert representations.

D. Input Assignment and Adaptivity

During training, each expert processes its designated region based on pre-defined ROIs. However, during inference, the gating module enables adaptive reliance on more confident experts. For instance, in low-resolution or occluded conditions, the gating module can learn to reduce the weight of the Eye Expert in favor of the Environmental module. This design will introduce a form of dynamic region prioritization, boosting robustness against partial occlusions, lighting changes, and attack diversity. Summary of results

Preliminary Results and Discussion Setup and Preliminary Results

As part of the ongoing development of the region-specific Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) framework, as illustrated in Figure 2, we have implemented and evaluated the Skin Liveness Expert module. This expert focuses on identifying presentation attacks by analyzing high-frequency skin texture descriptors and physiological inconsistencies. For this stage, we utilized the CASIA-FASD dataset for training, selecting relevant skin regions and applying preprocessing steps that included grayscale conversion and transformation into the YCbCr color space to enhance texture and chromatic features. Feature extraction was performed using Local Binary Patterns (LBP), and the extracted features were fed into various classifiers for evaluation, including Support Vector Machine (SVM), Random Forest, XGBoost, Logistic Regression, and a Fully Connected Neural Network (FCNN).

 Discussion

From Table II, the FCNN model achieved the best performance, reaching an accuracy of 97.20 %, precision of 97.10 %, recall of 97.00 %, and an F1-score of 97.05 %. on the other hand, Random Forest and XGBoost are close to 95 %, while SVM and Logistic Regression lag. This suggests that a small non-linear model works better with our LBP skin features than linear classifiers.

Moreover, the cross-dataset results of FCNN model (CASIA → NUAA) remains strong with accuracy 94.20%, Precision 94.35%, Recall 94.35%, and F1 94.14%, as illustrated in Table III. The findings illustrate the Skin Liveness Expert's ability in properly identifying fake skin textures in printed photos and video replay attacks.  Limitations and Future Work

This is a preliminary, concept-oriented study. Only the Skin Expert has been evaluated, the Eye and Environmental experts, as well as the adaptive fusion process, have yet to be developed. The evaluation is limited to CASIA-FASD and NUAA, and we did not provide PAD-standard metrics (e.g., APCER, BPCER, ACER, EER) or runtime/latency. Therefore, our future work will extend the evaluation across multiple datasets (e.g., Replay-Attack, CelebA-Spoof, FaceForensics++), integrate the remaining experts with adaptive fusion, and analyze computational efficiency to validate the feasibility of real-world deployment.

 Conclusion and Future Work

This paper introduced a conceptual, modular passive face liveness detection framework based on a region-specific Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architecture. By assigning distinct visual and behavioral cues to specialized expert modules, targeting the eye, skin, and environmental regions, the system aims to enhance generalization and robustness across diverse spoofing conditions. As of this work, only the Skin Liveness Expert has been implemented and evaluated. Preliminary results show effective destection of spoofed skin textures in print and replay attacks. The Eye and Environmental experts and the adaptive fusion layer remain under design. This proposal provides a solid foundation for a generalizable, modular system capable. Future work will focus on completing the full modular pipeline, benchmarking under cross-dataset and cross-attack settings and evaluating latency and model compression for real-time deployment.

Face liveness detection is essential for ensuring authenticity in electronic Know Your Customer (eKYC) and face recognition systems, particularly to prevent unauthorized access via presentation attacks (PAs). Passive methods, which require no user interaction, offer user-friendly solutions but often struggle to generalize across diverse PA types and environmental conditions. To address this challenge, this paper proposes a theoretical modular passive liveness detection framework based on a Region-Specific Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architecture, where each expert specializes in detecting specific liveness cues. The proposed framework is designed to include three experts, each focused on the eye region, skin texture, and environmental interaction. As a first step, this work presents the preliminary implementation and evaluation of the Skin Liveness Expert. The uniform LBP texture descriptors are extracted from skin regions and classified using various classifiers. On the CASIA-FASD dataset (in-domain), the best model Fully Connected Neural Network (FCNN) acieves an accuracy of 97.20 %, in cross-dataset evaluation (CASIA $\rightarrow$ NUAA), it achieves an accuracy of 94.20 %, indicating that region-specific texture-based cues transfer well. While the Eye Expert, Environmental Expert, and adaptive fusion mechanism remain under development, this paper establishes the foundation of a modular MoE framework with the potential to improve cross-dataset generalization and robustness against diverse attacks such as photos, replay videos, 3D masks, and deepfakes.

 Passive face liveness detection Mixture-of-Experts presentation attack detection cross-dataset generalization biometric security Introduction

The need for strong security frameworks has increased as biometric technologies, particularly facial recognition, are becoming integrated in sensitive areas like financial transactions, access control, and personal device security [1]. These systems are excellent at recognizing and authenticating people using their facial characteristics, but they are becoming more susceptible to spoofing or Presentation Attacks (PAs), in which attackers take advantage of easily accessible or fraudulent biometric data to obtain unauthorized access [2], [3]. These vulnerabilities pose significant risks, including financial fraud, identity theft, and breaches of privacy. Security measures such as Presentation Attack Detection (PAD) systems, which aim to identify and stop spoofing attacks, are vital in reducing these threats [4], [5]. Though face recognition systems are very good at identifying people based on facial features, they can't tell the difference between a real, live person (a genuine user) and a spoofing/presentation attacker (an imposter). PAD systems tackle this problem [6], [7]. Attackers frequently mimic authentic users by displaying a spoof face, a fake duplicate of a real user's face made using printed pictures, replay videos, 2D, 3D masks, or cosmetically manipulated faces, or digitally manipulated images (e.g., deepfakes) [4]. The inability to differentiate between live and fake faces emphasizes the significance of face liveness detection as a critical security element in current face recognition systems, particularly those employed for access control and authentication [1].

A face liveness detection system is one of the PAD techniques, attempting to solve this issue by spotting PAs and guaranteeing that only live faces can access protected systems [1], [8], [9]. These algorithms can efficiently classify spoofing attempts when PAs are detected in conditions similar to those on which the system was trained [10]. However, such systems perform much worse in unconstrained conditions, new attack types, or cross-database settings, where environmental characteristics like illumination, camera quality, and human variety change from training circumstances [11], [12]. This restriction exposes a fundamental flaw in the reliability and scalability of current face liveness detection algorithms.

Existing face, or in general, liveness detection techniques are divided into two categories: active and passive [13]. Active methods need the user to do specified actions, such as blinking or nodding, to indicate their existence [14]. While successful, these solutions degrade the user experience and are not suitable for real-time or hands-free applications [13]. In contrast, passive methods may detect liveness from small facial and environmental cues alone, without requiring any interaction from the user [15]. While passive solutions are user-friendly and easy to implement, they often struggle in dynamic or unstructured environments [16]. They are especially vulnerable to sophisticated spoofing methods, such as AI-generated deepfakes and high-resolution 3D masks, which may provide very realistic and misleading biometric representations [17], [18]. As fraudsters use advanced technology, the fabricated identities they create become more realistic and complex, presenting substantial difficulties to the security and integrity of biometric systems. Traditional detection approaches, which were not intended to handle these developing threats, frequently fail, compromising the security and integrity of face recognition systems [8].

Despite significant advancements in passive face liveness detection technologies, current solutions face persistent challenges in balancing accuracy, real-time processing, and generalization, especially in the face of increasingly sophisticated PAs. These limitations have particular significance in uncontrolled environments, where factors such as lighting, camera quality, and subject diversity vary significantly from training conditions. Monolithic models often fail to generalize across such variability, leading to decreased robustness and reliability.

This research aims to address these challenges by proposing a theoretical and conceptual modular passive face liveness detection framework based on a region-specific Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architecture, which specializes different facial regions and cues to particular experts to improve generalization. The primary aim of this research is to develop an optimised passive facial liveness detection system that effectively distinguishes live faces from spoofed ones, including complex attacks, like 3D masks and deepfakes, while maintaining computational efficiency.

The paper is structured into five sections. Section I introduces the research, detailing the context, aims, and objectives. Section II offers an examination of related research work in passive face liveness detection research. Section III describes the proposed approach, specifying the framework and design. Section IV presents the preliminary experimental results from the initial module implementation. Lastly, Section V concludes the paper with a summary of findings and future work.

 Related Work

This section discusses related research on passive facial liveness detection methods, emphasizing advancements from recent years. It examines studies that focus on PAD mechanisms to secure biometric systems against Facial PA, particularly through passive facial liveness detection techniques that do not require user interaction. The section summarizes research papers on passive facial liveness detection methods (non-AI-based and AI-based) and the dataset.

Passive Face Liveness Detection Techniques

Passive face liveness detection (PAD) aims to distinguish between live and spoofed faces without requiring user interaction. Traditional methods relied on handcrafted features like Local Binary Patterns (LBP) [10], [19], [20], motion cues [17], or frequency analysis and Fourier transforms [20], [21]. These techniques were often combined with threshold-based [10], [17], [20]–[23] or classical machine learning classifiers like SVMs [24]–[26], logistic regression [27], and other approaches [19].

With the rise of deep learning, CNN-based models became popular due to their ability to learn discriminative features directly from raw images [16], [18], [28]–[32]. Hybrid models combining CNNs and LSTMs [33], Hybrid models with other deep and machine learning models, ResNet, and SVM [34] or transfer learning [35], or lightweight architectures [36]–[38] such as LivenessNet [15], MobileNetV2 [39] have demonstrated improved accuracy.

However, these models often suffer from limited generalizability across datasets due to their monolithic design, which fails to adapt to varying spoofing conditions such as lighting, device quality, or attack type.

 Mixture-of-Experts (Moe) Approaches

While most face liveness or PAs detection systems adopt monolithic models, a few studies have explored modular or expert-based strategies to improve generalization and robustness against diverse PAs conditions.

One of the earliest works is by Kollreider et al. [40], who proposed a liveness verification system that integrates multiple handcrafted experts. Their system combines 3D motion cues and eye-blinking detection using optical flow and velocity analysis, all without relying on specialized hardware. Though pioneering for its time, this approach is non-AI and relies on rigid rule-based thresholds, limiting adaptability to more sophisticated and unseen attacks.

In recent years, there has been a shift toward AI-based Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architectures. Notably, Zhou et al. [41] introduced an Adaptive Mixture of Experts Learning (AMEL) framework, where each expert is trained on a specific dataset (e.g., CASIA, MSU) to capture domain-specific spoofing patterns. A Dynamic Expert Aggregation (DEA) module combines these expert outputs based on relevance to the test sample. Furthermore, a meta-learning strategy simulates unseen domains, enabling strong generalization across datasets. While AMEL achieves excellent performance (e.g., 11.31 % HTER on unseen OULU), it assumes access to clearly separable domain-specific training data, and its relevance scoring may degrade if domain similarity is misestimated. Moreover, the study focuses only on physical PAs types, it doesn't consider digitally face-manipulated attack types.

Another study by Chen et al. [42] proposed MoAE-CR (Mixture-of-Attack-Experts with Class Regularization) for unified detection of both physical and digital face attacks. This model employs soft expert routing and class regularization modules to handle intra-class variance (e.g., various spoof types) and inter-class similarity (e.g., live vs. highly realistic attacks). Despite achieving state-of-the-art results on complex datasets like UniAttackData and JFSFDB, this architecture is computationally heavy and lacks analysis of real-time inference. A different perspective is offered by a study [43] on face forgery detection (digital face manipulations, not strictly liveness detection) that used Adaptive Learning From Experts (ALFE). Each expert is trained on a specific forgery type (e.g., Deepfakes, FaceSwap), and a student model learns to distill knowledge adaptively based on prediction confidence. Although effective for forgery classification, this work is task-specific and does not address live vs. spoof discrimination, which is central to PAD. Another study focused on digital PAs [44], the authors introduced the Deepfake Mixture of Experts (DFMoE) model, a Heterogeneous Mixture of Experts (HMoE) model. Their architecture employs a dynamic gating network to select and combine expert subnetworks of varying capacities and scales based on input characteristics. By coupling expert specialization with adaptive data augmentation, DFMoE enhances robustness against both known and unseen forgery types. Related direction utilizes feature fusion with specialized Vision Transformer (ViTs) encoders [45]. The authors employ three distinct ViTs, DaViT, iFormer, and GPViT, each focusing on capturing global context, frequency domain artifacts, and high-resolution details, respectively. These are merged via an MLP-Mixer fusion module, achieving state-of-the-art deepfake detection performance across FaceForensics ++ and Celeb-DF benchmarks. While not using expert routing, this method demonstrates the power of modality-specialized feature processing in forgery detection.

Unlike previous works, as summarized in Table I, our proposed model introduces a conceptual modular framework, a region-specific MoE framework for passive face liveness detection. Instead of specializing experts by domain or attack type, which often limits flexibility when encountering new or mixed spoofing methods, we design experts that each focus on a specific visual cue, such as eye region, skin texture, or environmental consistency. This allows our model to capture diverse liveness indicators in parallel, enhancing generalization across unseen attacks, and reducing dependence on curated datasets.

 Methodology and System Design

This study proposes a novel conceptual modular, passive face liveness detection system using a region-specific MoE architecture. The architecture decomposes the detection task into three parallel expert modules, where the design of each expert module specializes in a specific visual or behavioral core module: Eye Liveness expert, Skin Liveness expert, and Environmental expert. This region-driven modularization approach was chosen to improve the system's ability to generalize across diverse face PA scenarios and environments. By isolating distinct tasks into specialized modules, the system can focus on specific abnormal features present in different spoofing methods, such as the lack of natural blinking in video attacks or unrealistic skin textures in maskbased attacks. This enables targeted detection strategies that increase the overall robustness of the system.

Architecture Overview

As illustrated in Figure 1, the system comprises three core components:

Expert Modules: Each expert is designed as a lightweight neural subnetwork that learns specialized features from its corresponding Region of Interest (ROI).

Gating Network: A high-level context-aware gating module would dynamically modulate the contribution weights of each expert's output based on input characteristics.

Fusion & Classifier: Expert outputs would be fused through a soft attention mechanism or gated summation, producing a final liveness score.

Overview of the proposed region-specific mixture-of-experts (moe) framework for passive face liveness detection.

Expert Modules and Learning Objectives

Each expert learns to encode region-specific liveness cues by optimizing task-driven objectives over localized input features.

The Eye Expert learns temporal motion patterns using 3D convolutional blocks or stacked LSTMs/CNNs. Supervisory signals are driven by blink occurrence, gaze deviation, and inter-frame motion entropy. This module is particularly sensitive to replay attacks and static image spoofs.

The Skin Expert focuses on high-frequency texture descriptors (e.g., pores, specular reflections). It is trained using patch-level classification losses, texture regularization, and spatial smoothness constraints. This module focuses on detecting presentation artifacts in printed images and silicone masks.

The Environmental Expert analyzes global consistency between the facial foreground and the environmental background. Features include shadow coherence, background motion, and illumination dynamics. It uses regression and contrastive losses to model natural scene cues and detect compositional inconsistencies often present in digital or recombined inputs.

Stepwise development process for proposed region-specific mixture-of-experts (moe) architecture for passive face liveness detection.

All experts operate independently and may share weights in initial convolutional layers to reduce model size.

 Adaptive Fusion Mechanism

Unlike traditional MoE models that perform hard routing, we adopt a soft routing mechanism via a gating network. The gating module receives a global context vector (e.g., pooled features from the shared encoder or a separate attention summary) and outputs a soft weight vector $w=[w 1, w 2, w 3]$ via a softmax layer. These weights determine the contribution of each expert: \begin{equation*} F_{\text {fused }}=\sum_{i=1}^{3} w_{i} \cdot F_{i} \tag{1} \end{equation*} where $F_{i}$ denotes the feature vector from the $i^{\text {th }}$ expert, and $w_{i}$ is its corresponding weight produced by the gating network. The gating module will be jointly trained with the experts using a combined loss function defined in Equation (2). \begin{equation*} L_{\text {total }}=L_{\text {cls }}+\lambda \cdot L_{\text {gate-reg }}+\mu \cdot L_{\text {expert-indep }} \tag{2} \end{equation*}

$L_{\text {total }}$: binary cross-entropy loss for final liveness classification.

$L_{\text {gate-reg: }}$: optional entropy regularization to encourage confident gating decisions.

$L_{\text {expert-indep }}$: optional penalty to encourage diversity in expert representations.

D. Input Assignment and Adaptivity

During training, each expert processes its designated region based on pre-defined ROIs. However, during inference, the gating module enables adaptive reliance on more confident experts. For instance, in low-resolution or occluded conditions, the gating module can learn to reduce the weight of the Eye Expert in favor of the Environmental module. This design will introduce a form of dynamic region prioritization, boosting robustness against partial occlusions, lighting changes, and attack diversity. Summary of results

Preliminary Results and Discussion Setup and Preliminary Results

As part of the ongoing development of the region-specific Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) framework, as illustrated in Figure 2, we have implemented and evaluated the Skin Liveness Expert module. This expert focuses on identifying presentation attacks by analyzing high-frequency skin texture descriptors and physiological inconsistencies. For this stage, we utilized the CASIA-FASD dataset for training, selecting relevant skin regions and applying preprocessing steps that included grayscale conversion and transformation into the YCbCr color space to enhance texture and chromatic features. Feature extraction was performed using Local Binary Patterns (LBP), and the extracted features were fed into various classifiers for evaluation, including Support Vector Machine (SVM), Random Forest, XGBoost, Logistic Regression, and a Fully Connected Neural Network (FCNN).

 Discussion

From Table II, the FCNN model achieved the best performance, reaching an accuracy of 97.20 %, precision of 97.10 %, recall of 97.00 %, and an F1-score of 97.05 %. on the other hand, Random Forest and XGBoost are close to 95 %, while SVM and Logistic Regression lag. This suggests that a small non-linear model works better with our LBP skin features than linear classifiers.

Moreover, the cross-dataset results of FCNN model (CASIA → NUAA) remains strong with accuracy 94.20%, Precision 94.35%, Recall 94.35%, and F1 94.14%, as illustrated in Table III. The findings illustrate the Skin Liveness Expert's ability in properly identifying fake skin textures in printed photos and video replay attacks. Furthermore, it demonstrates that concentrating on skin texture indicators retains valuable data despite changes in the dataset, so validating the effectiveness of region-specific analysis in passive liveness detection. Limitations and Future Work

This is a preliminary, concept-oriented study. Only the Skin Expert has been evaluated, the Eye and Environmental experts, as well as the adaptive fusion process, have yet to be developed. The evaluation is limited to CASIA-FASD and NUAA, and we did not provide PAD-standard metrics (e.g., APCER, BPCER, ACER, EER) or runtime/latency. Therefore, our future work will extend the evaluation across multiple datasets (e.g., Replay-Attack, CelebA-Spoof, FaceForensics++), integrate the remaining experts with adaptive fusion, and analyze computational efficiency to validate the feasibility of real-world deployment.

 Conclusion and Future Work

This paper introduced a conceptual, modular passive face liveness detection framework based on a region-specific Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architecture. By assigning distinct visual and behavioral cues to specialized expert modules, targeting the eye, skin, and environmental regions, the system aims to enhance generalization and robustness across diverse spoofing conditions. As of this work, only the Skin Liveness Expert has been implemented and evaluated. Preliminary results show effective destection of spoofed skin textures in print and replay attacks. The Eye and Environmental experts and the adaptive fusion layer remain under design. This proposal provides a solid foundation for a generalizable, modular system capable. Future work will focus on completing the full modular pipeline, benchmarking under cross-dataset and cross-attack settings and evaluating latency and model compression for real-time deployment.
