Over the past few years, the use of electric scooters has increased significantly. According to the latest data, there are about $\mathbf{1 0 0, 0 0 0}$ electric scooters in Bulgaria. Accidents with this type of vehicle are constantly increasing, with serious accidents for the first half of 2025. are about 470, of which 8 are fatal [7]. This article presents a study showing the possibilities for effective braking of the described vehicles. The maximum braking deceleration, average braking deceleration, braking distance and other specific parameters in an emergency braking situation are examined.

 Brake deceleration electric scooter brake distance Introduction

The speed of the vehicle is directly related to its braking time and, accordingly, to the braking deceleration. At higher speeds, the braking distance is longer. This is because the distance a vehicle has to travel to stop increases as its speed increases [1], [4]. The speed of the vehicle is determined according to the distance traveled, because the two values are directly related. Determining the speed according to the road traveled by the vehicle is the most common way to determine it in expert practice.

Braking lag is actually deceleration. It depends on several factors: the speed of the car, road conditions, weather conditions, grip and condition of the tires, the condition of the braking system, the behavior and reaction of the driver [3], [5].

Auto technical expertise is the process of examining and evaluating a vehicle, its components and systems in order to determine the cause of the accident. Braking deceleration is one of the key parameters that are taken into account in these evaluations. The correct selection of braking deceleration and its measurement is extremely important for the correct conclusions in expert analysis. There are several factors that affect the length of the braking distance and, accordingly, the braking deceleration:

Speed: The higher the speed of the vehicle, the longer the braking distance. This is because it takes more time and distance to slow down;

Road conditions: The presence of water, dust or other substances can significantly increase braking distance, as it reduces traction between the tires and the road;

The speed of electric scooters can vary from $25 ~\text{km} / \mathrm{h}$ to over $55 ~\text{km} / \mathrm{h}$. The electric scooter is characterized by an electric motor, battery, folding design, lights and brakes. The power of the electric motor affects acceleration and the ability to overcome slopes. The battery determines the range. LED headlights and brake lights increase safety. Disc and electric brakes provide effective braking. Electric scooters usually have disc brakes and electric brakes that provide reliable braking.

The brakes of electric scooters are of the following types:

1.Foot brake - it is activated by pressing with your foot and acts on the rear wheel (older or budget model). This system has a simple design, no control electronics, but allows less control over the scooter when braking, and stability is dependent on the change in the position of the body in a transverse direction.

2.Drum brakes - a variation of mechanical braking systems, but use a brake drum mounted on the wheel of the wheel. These systems have a simple design and long-term maintenance. Compared to disc brake systems, they have lower efficiency and are sensitive to moisture and water.

3.Mechanical disc brakes - brake discs, usually actuated by levers, mechanically. This braking system provides high-quality and predictable braking, good handling on dry and wet surfaces.

4.Hydraulic disc brakes - hydraulic brake systems are more effective by providing more braking force on the brake discs by applying less force on the driver's side. As a result, less lever travel is obtained, stable braking under different conditions.

5.Brake by energy regeneration (to motor braking) - in the process of braking, the electric motor is in generator mode, creates resistance to the drive wheel, creates energy through which it charges the battery. It is activated via the brake lever or through the regeneration mode. It helps to gradually reduce speed, but is not effective when driving at high speeds.

6.Combined braking systems - combine braking by energy regeneration with mechanical or hydraulic brakes for better control of the vehicle when braking and increase braking performance. These systems offer smooth braking, combined with less wear on the friction surfaces, as well as increased durability.

The purpose of this study is to investigate and determine in the conditions of emergency braking - the braking deceleration, braking distance and the parameters in the process of braking electric scooters at speeds of $20 ~\text{km} / \mathrm{h}$ and $30 ~\text{km} / \mathrm{h}$.

 Research Methodology Used Apparatus

Braking deceleration can be determined experimentally in several ways [2], [6]:

By accelerometer (g - test) - The deceleration is measured through a specially made sensor, most often inertial. This method requires special and expensive equipment, which makes it difficult to apply in individual cases of road traffic accident (RTA);

Through the fifth wheel - a specially made wheel is attached to the vehicle, through which the distance traveled is measured. By differentiating the dependence of the path on time, speed and acceleration are obtained. The method is difficult to apply to certain cases, due to the inconvenience of installing the mechanism on the tested vehicles;

By measuring the braking time - the braking time is measured from the moment the brake system is activated to its braking, the speed is measured with radar;

With the help of a sled - a specially made sled with a rubber pad is pulled, measuring the pulling force through a dynamometer, which is the frictional force between the sled and the road surface. This method is too inaccurate, given that it does not take into account the peculiarities of the dynamics of the braking process of the vehicle, the influence of tires and the redistribution of weight on the wheels.

In the studies to determine the braking parameters, a device equipped with an accelerometer and the possibility of mounting the examined vehicles is used.

The meter used is shown in Figure 1, indicating its characteristics and functions.

Apparatus

The BRAKE TESTER is designed to measure brake deceleration for a very wide range of vehicles, including: cars, trucks, motorcycles, two- and four-wheeled vehicles, etc. The device allows you to record dynamic phenomena of the braking process. The parameters that are recorded are: maximum braking deceleration, average braking deceleration, speed at the start of braking, braking distance, etc. The device is suitable for measuring all kinds of brake systems, mechanical and hydraulic.

The principle of operation of the Brake Tester is based on measuring acceleration through the latest generation of acceleration sensor, manufactured by an American company (Analog Devices). This sensor can measure accelerations in the range from -1.7 g to +1.7 g with a resolution of 0.01 g. The sensor readings are recorded in the memory of the metering system of the device with a frequency of 100 Hz, which allows subsequent processing of the received data. The internal memory of the device allows you to store values of up to 30 measurements with 2000 measurement attempts. The device's timer records the following data:

Date of measurement - day, month and year;

Exact time to start the brake test;

Accurate measurement duration [s];

Exact suspension duration [s];

Deceleration $\left[\mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}^{2}\right]$;

Maximum deceleration $\left[\mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}^{2}\right]$;

Average deceleration $\left[\mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}^{2}\right]$;

Speed of the tested vehicle at the moment of braking [km/h];

Braking distance length [mb];

Total length of distance traveled by the vehicle [mb];

Brake pedal pressure [daN];

Maximum pressure on the brake pedal [daN]. The measuring system is equipped with a backup data memory battery.

A built-in communication connection allows the results after the measurement to be transferred to a PC for subsequent processing. Thanks to the use of an innovative data processing algorithm, the device automatically eliminates the harmful effect of tilting the vehicle frame from the horizontal during braking, thanks to which the graphs of the distance traveled, speed and deceleration are automatically corrected through the use of special software.

Conclusions

For an electric scooter when braking from a speed of $20 ~\text{km} / \mathrm{h}$ with front brake, the maximum braking deceleration is $4.38 ~\mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}^{2}$, when braking with rear brake, the maximum braking deceleration is $4.86 ~\mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}^{2}$, when braking with both brakes applied, the maximum braking deceleration is $6.25 ~\mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}^{2}$.

When braking from a speed of $30 ~\text{km} / \mathrm{h}$ with the front brake, the maximum braking deceleration is $4,29 ~\mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}^{2}$, when braking with rear brake, the maximum braking deceleration is $5,57 ~\mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}^{2}$, when braking with both brakes applied, the maximum braking deceleration is $6,93 ~\mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}^{2}$.

The average maximum braking deceleration for an electric scooter when braking with front brake is $4,34 ~\mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}^{2}$, when braking with rear braking, the maximum average braking deceleration is $5,21 ~\mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}^{2}$, when braking with both brakes applied, the average maximum braking deceleration is $6,59 ~\mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}^{2}$.

#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""
Convert XML input-output pairs to JSONL format for Google Vertex AI fine-tuning

Usage:
    python convert_xml_to_jsonl.py

Requirements:
    - Put all input XMLs in folder: ./xml-input/
    - Put all output XMLs in folder: ./xml-output/
    - Files can have patterns like: 
      * Input: 11273196_ft_Input.xml → Output: 11273196_ft_output.xml
      * Input: file_001.xml → Output: file_001.xml (matching names)

Output:
    - Creates: training_data.jsonl (ready for Vertex AI upload)
"""

import json
import os
from pathlib import Path
import re
import sys

def extract_file_id(filename):
    """Extract ID from filename for matching input/output pairs"""
    # Try to extract numeric ID or base name
    # Pattern 1: 11273196_ft_Input.xml -> extract 11273196
    match = re.match(r'(\d+)_ft_', filename)
    if match:
        return match.group(1)
    
    # Pattern 2: file_001.xml -> extract 001
    match = re.match(r'file_(\d+)', filename)
    if match:
        return match.group(1)
    
    # Pattern 3: sample_001.xml -> extract 001
    match = re.match(r'\w+_(\d+)', filename)
    if match:
        return match.group(1)
    
    # If no pattern, return filename without extension
    return os.path.splitext(filename)[0]

def find_matching_output(input_filename, output_dir):
    """Find matching output file for given input file"""
    input_id = extract_file_id(input_filename)
    
    output_files = [f for f in os.listdir(output_dir) if f.endswith('.xml')]
    
    # First try exact match
    if input_filename in output_files:
        return input_filename
    
    # Try to find matching output by ID
    for output_file in output_files:
        output_id = extract_file_id(output_file)
        if output_id == input_id:
            return output_file
    
    return None

def create_jsonl_from_xml_pairs(input_dir="xml-input", output_dir="xml-output", output_file="training_data.jsonl"):
    """
    Convert XML input-output pairs to JSONL format for Vertex AI fine-tuning
    
    Args:
        input_dir: Directory containing input XML files
        output_dir: Directory containing output XML files
        output_file: Output JSONL filename
    """
    
    # Check if directories exist
    if not os.path.exists(input_dir):
        print(f"❌ Error: Input directory '{input_dir}' not found!")
        print(f"   Create folder: {os.path.abspath(input_dir)}")
        return False
    
    if not os.path.exists(output_dir):
        print(f"❌ Error: Output directory '{output_dir}' not found!")
        print(f"   Create folder: {os.path.abspath(output_dir)}")
        return False
    
    # Get input XML files
    input_files = sorted([f for f in os.listdir(input_dir) if f.endswith('.xml')])
    
    if not input_files:
        print(f"❌ Error: No XML files found in '{input_dir}'")
        return False
    
    print(f"📂 Found {len(input_files)} input XML files")
    
    training_examples = []
    skipped = 0
    
    for idx, filename in enumerate(input_files, 1):
        input_path = os.path.join(input_dir, filename)
        
        # Find matching output file (handles different naming patterns)
        output_filename = find_matching_output(filename, output_dir)
        
        if not output_filename:
            print(f"⚠️  Skipping {filename} - no matching output file found")
            skipped += 1
            continue
        
        output_path = os.path.join(output_dir, output_filename)
        
        # Read input XML
        try:
            with open(input_path, 'r', encoding='utf-8') as f:
                input_xml = f.read().strip()
        except Exception as e:
            print(f"⚠️  Error reading {input_path}: {e}")
            skipped += 1
            continue
        
        # Read output XML
        try:
            with open(output_path, 'r', encoding='utf-8') as f:
                output_xml = f.read().strip()
        except Exception as e:
            print(f"⚠️  Error reading {output_path}: {e}")
            skipped += 1
            continue
        
        # Create JSONL example for Vertex AI
        # Format: {"prompt": "user instruction", "completion": "model response"}
        example = {
            "prompt": f"Transform and tag this XML document with proper references and table names:\n\n{input_xml}",
            "completion": f"\n{output_xml}"
        }
        
        training_examples.append(example)
        
        # Print progress
        input_size = len(input_xml) / 1024
        output_size = len(output_xml) / 1024
        print(f"✓ {idx}. {filename} → {output_filename} ({input_size:.1f}KB → {output_size:.1f}KB)")
    
    if not training_examples:
        print("❌ Error: No training examples created!")
        return False
    
    # Write JSONL file
    try:
        with open(output_file, 'w', encoding='utf-8') as f:
            for example in training_examples:
                f.write(json.dumps(example, ensure_ascii=False) + '\n')
        
        print(f"\n✅ SUCCESS: Created {output_file}")
        print(f"📊 Statistics:")
        print(f"   - Total examples: {len(training_examples)}")
        print(f"   - Skipped: {skipped}")
        print(f"   - File size: {os.path.getsize(output_file) / (1024*1024):.2f} MB")
        
        # Show first example
        print(f"\n📝 First example in JSONL:")
        first_example = training_examples[0]
        print(f"   Prompt length: {len(first_example['prompt'])} chars")
        print(f"   Completion length: {len(first_example['completion'])} chars")
        
        return True
        
    except Exception as e:
        print(f"❌ Error writing JSONL file: {e}")
        return False

def verify_jsonl_file(jsonl_file="training_data.jsonl"):
    """Verify JSONL file format"""
    
    if not os.path.exists(jsonl_file):
        print(f"❌ File not found: {jsonl_file}")
        return False
    
    print(f"\n🔍 Verifying {jsonl_file}...")
    
    try:
        with open(jsonl_file, 'r', encoding='utf-8') as f:
            lines = f.readlines()
        
        print(f"   ✓ Total lines: {len(lines)}")
        
        # Check each line is valid JSON
        valid_count = 0
        for i, line in enumerate(lines, 1):
            try:
                example = json.loads(line)
                if "prompt" in example and "completion" in example:
                    valid_count += 1
                else:
                    print(f"   ⚠️  Line {i}: Missing 'prompt' or 'completion' field")
            except json.JSONDecodeError as e:
                print(f"   ⚠️  Line {i}: Invalid JSON - {e}")
        
        print(f"   ✓ Valid examples: {valid_count}/{len(lines)}")
        
        if valid_count == len(lines):
            print(f"✅ JSONL file is valid and ready for Vertex AI upload!")
            return True
        else:
            print(f"⚠️  Some examples are invalid")
            return False
            
    except Exception as e:
        print(f"❌ Error verifying file: {e}")
        return False

if __name__ == "__main__":
    print("=" * 60)
    print("XML to JSONL Converter for Google Vertex AI Fine-tuning")
    print("=" * 60)
    
    # Create directories if they don't exist
    os.makedirs("xml-input", exist_ok=True)
    os.makedirs("xml-output", exist_ok=True)
    
    print("\n📋 Instructions:")
    print("   1. Place all INPUT XMLs in: ./xml-input/")
    print("   2. Place all OUTPUT XMLs in: ./xml-output/")
    print("   3. Naming patterns supported:")
    print("      - 11273196_ft_Input.xml ↔ 11273196_ft_output.xml")
    print("      - file_001.xml ↔ file_001.xml (same name)")
    print("   4. Run this script\n")
    
    # Convert XMLs to JSONL
    success = create_jsonl_from_xml_pairs(
        input_dir="xml-input",
        output_dir="xml-output",
        output_file="training_data.jsonl"
    )
    
    if success:
        # Verify JSONL file
        verify_jsonl_file("training_data.jsonl")
        
        print("\n" + "=" * 60)
        print("📤 NEXT STEPS:")
        print("=" * 60)
        print("1. Go to: https://console.cloud.google.com/vertex-ai")
        print("2. Click: Generative AI Studio")
        print("3. Click: Create tuned model")
        print("4. Select: Gemini 2.0 Flash")
        print("5. Upload: training_data.jsonl")
        print("6. Set epochs to: 3")
        print("7. Click: Start Tuning")
        print("=" * 60 + "\n")
    else:
        print("\n❌ Failed to create JSONL. Check the errors above.")
        sys.exit(1)

This work explores the utilization of Kubernetes' (HPA) and (VPA) mechanisms to enhance energy efficiency in server infrastructures. The work proposes a merged approach of Kubernetes scheduling techniques to optimize resource allocation based on different workload requirements. The objective is to achieve energy-efficient solutions that improve performance and reduce cost liabilities for organizations. The methodology includes a two-dimensional bin packing problem for long-running services, where pods are allocated with the least number of cluster nodes based on CPU and memory requests. The runtime monitor fields on nodes help group applications with comparable runtime and forecast resource use until the subsequent scaling cycle. Autoscaling techniques, including HPA and VPA, are implemented for long-term processing applications. While VPA modifies the resource requests and limits of containers inside of already-existing pods, HPA modifies the number of replicas based on CPU utilization or custom metrics. The study highlights the difference between HPA and VPA in terms of capacity adjustments. While HPA scales by creating or deleting pods, VPA scales by dynamically altering the CPU or memory in the current pod containers. The work emphasizes synergy between VPA and HPA, where VPA calculates and adjusts the appropriate resource allocation, while HPA scale pods horizontally using custom metrics. The work explores the influence of container and virtual machine (VM) differences on enhancing energy efficiency through Kubernetes' HPA and VPA mechanisms. It delves into how these mechanisms address resource allocation challenges and optimize energy consumption. The paper concludes by highlighting the potential of software-based solutions, specifically Kubernetes, for improving energy efficiency in server infrastructures.

 Kubernetes metrics-server energy efficiency resource allocation autoscaling Introduction

In the modern digital landscape, the demand for energyefficient server infrastructures has become increasingly significant [1]. As organizations strive to optimize resource utilization and reduce operational costs, software-based solutions play a pivotal role in achieving sustainable and environmentally conscious server management [2], [3]. In this study, we explore the utilization of Kubernetes, a widely recognized container orchestration system, to improve energy efficiency [4]. Our focus is on effectively employing the (HPA) and (VPA) functionalities within Kubernetes for this purpose [5], [6]. To automate the deployment, scaling, and maintenance of containerized applications, Kubernetes is a powerful solution. By leveraging its built-in features and extensible architecture, organizations can streamline their server operations while minimizing energy consumption. The HPA and VPA mechanisms within Kubernetes offer flexible options for dynamically adjusting resource allocation based on workload demands, thus enabling organizations to establish a balance between effectiveness and energy conservation.

In context of server infrastructures, the characteristics of containers and virtual machines (VMs) influence the efficacy of energy-efficient practices [7]. Containers provide lightweight and isolated runtime environments, allowing for efficient resource utilization. On the other hand, VMs offer hardwarelevel virtualization, enabling fine-grained control over resource allocation but potentially incurring higher overhead costs. Understanding the implications of these differences is crucial in developing strategies that maximize energy efficiency while ensuring optimal application performance. The difference of containers and virtual machines is shown in Fig. 1

Difference between vms and containers

The objective of this research is to propose a merged approach of Kubernetes scheduling techniques that harnesses the power of HPA and VPA to optimize resource allocation and enhance energy efficiency. Our methodology revolves around two key aspects: scheduling and autoscaling. Regarding enduring services, we approach the processes for scheduling as a twodimensional bin packing problem, carefully allocating pods to minimize the number of cluster nodes required. Based on the unique requirements of each task, memory requests are given a higher priority than CPU demands.

To address workload fluctuations and varying resource demands, autoscaling techniques are employed. Both HPA and VPA play pivotal roles in this regard. HPA dynamically adjusts the number of pod replicas based on CPU utilization or custom-defined metrics, ensuring that the application scales horizontally to meet demand. VPA, on the other hand, focuses on optimizing resource requests and limits within existing pod containers, providing granular control over CPU and memory allocation.

By leveraging the complementary capabilities of VPA and HPA, we aim to achieve optimal energy efficiency in server infrastructures. VPA ensures each pod receives a set amount of resources, over historical usage patterns and workload requirements. Meanwhile, HPA scales the application horizontally, dynamically adapting the number of pod replicas to match the workload demand and maximize resource utilization. This research paper presents a comprehensive exploration of Kubernetes' HPA and VPA mechanisms in the context of enhancing energy efficiency. By leveraging these features alongside care-ful scheduling and autoscaling strategies, organizations can effectively optimize resource allocation, reduce operational costs, and contribute to sustainable server infrastructures.

 Literature Review

Kubernetes Resource Management: Kubernetes is an opensource container orchestration solution that provides excellent distributed resource management. It provides computational optimization capabilities such as resource allocation, scalability, and scheduling [8]. Kubernetes defines application requirements using declaration models, which then automatically regulate resource allocations and policies based on these requirements. Vertical Pod Autoscaler (VPA): Vertical Pod Autoscaler (VPA) is a Kubernetes component that changes the resource needs and constraints of individual containers in a pod based on historical use patterns. VPA assesses the resource utilization of pods and advises or automatically updates their resource settings to ensure effective resource usage [9]. The rapid deployment of generative Artificial Intelligence (GenAI) systems has introduced a new class of technical privacy risks that exceed the scope of traditional data protection frameworks. This paper investigates these emerging vulnerabilities through the lens of the OWASP Top 10 for Large Language Models (LLMs) - 2025 edition, correlating them with real-world privacy failures from $2023-2025$. It categorizes incidents such as prompt injection, model poisoning, excessive agency, and improper output handling, drawing connections between architectural design choices and user-level data exposure. High-profile cases - including OpenAI's GDPR fine by Italy's Garante, the Hugging Face transformer compromise, and the Reddit-Anthropic dispute illustrate the systemic nature of GenAI risks. In parallel, the study evaluates international regulatory and governance responses, including the EU AI Act, NIST AI RMF, ISO/IEC 42001, and China's algorithmic controls, highlighting their strengths and implementation gaps. The analysis reveals that technical vulnerabilities often cascade across the GenAI lifecycle, creating enforcement blind spots and compliance challenges. The paper concludes by advocating for a holistic, cross-functional risk management strategy that integrates secure-by-design principles, real-time oversight, and proactive alignment with evolving regulatory standards.

 GenerativeAI Technical privacy risks OWASP \DeclareUnicodeCharacter{20AC}{\ifmmode\text{\euro}\else\euro\fi} Introduction

In December 2024, Italy's data protection authority, Garante per la Protezione dei Dati Personali, imposed a 15 million euro fine on OpenAI - the first penalty of its kind against a generative AI provider - for violations of the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR). The decision originates from an ex officio investigation initiated following the publication of press-reports regarding a data breach that occurred on March 2023. [1] Concerns included the absence of a valid legal basis for processing personal data, inadequate transparency in data handling, and insufficient safeguards for minors. This landmark enforcement - complemented by a mandated six-month public awareness campaign - signals the growing confrontation between rapid AI innovation and privacy safeguards. It highlights pervasive vulnerabilities in how Generative Artificial Intelligence (GenAI) systems harvest and handle personal data.

While previous studies have focused primarily on ethical and legal implications by authors like Feuerriegel et al., 2024 [2] and Golda et al., 2024 [5], comparatively little attention has been given to the technical foundations of these risks how specific system vulnerabilities enable privacy breaches in real-world applications. Recent research of Lyu et al., 2025 [19] has begun to systematize the understanding of such risks by mapping privacy attacks across the large language model (LLM) lifecycle, yet the connection between these technical vulnerabilities and regulatory or governance frameworks remains underexplored. This gap underscores the need for systematic analysis linking the technical characteristics of GenAI systems to their compliance, accountability, and oversight implications.

Accordingly, this study examines the evolving landscape of technical privacy risks in generative AI by analyzing incidents reported between 2023 and 2025 and aligning them with established security taxonomies such as the Open Worldwide Application Security Project (OWASP) Top 10 for Large Language Models (LLMs). By correlating these vulnerabilities with high-profile privacy failures and evaluating the corresponding international governance responses - particularly the European Union Artificial Intelligence Act (EU AI Act), the National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST) Artificial Intelligence Risk Management Framework (AI RMF), and the International Organization for Standardization - International Electrotechnical Commission (ISO/IEC) 42001 - the paper aims to identify the structural weaknesses that perpetuate enforcement gaps.

The overarching objective is to contribute to a more integrated understanding of GenAI privacy risks, bridging the divide between technical security practices and regulatory expectations, and supporting the development of secure-bydesign, accountable AI systems.

 Architecturial Foundations of Generative Ai (Gen Ai)

The roots of artificial intelligence (AI) trace back to mid20th century philosophical and computational ideas, including Alan Turing's seminal exploration "Computing Machinery and Intelligence" and the Dartmouth Conference of 1956 organized by John McCarthy and Marvin Minsky. These developments formally established AI as a scientific field. Early symbolic reasoning systems such as ELIZA (1966) marked the nascent stages of computer-based language simulation. The 1980s saw renewed interest in neural networks, though progress was impeded by computational limitations and periods of reduced funding-often referred to as "AI winters." Despite these challenges, momentum returned in the 1990s and early 2000s as machine learning methods matured.

Most notably, the introduction of AlexNet in 2012 revolutionized image recognition and heralded the era of deep learning. Further breakthroughs followed with the publication of the transformer architecture in 2017, enabling efficient and scalable sequence modeling via self-attention. These advancements set the stage for large language models (LLMs)

such as GPT 3 and GPT 4, with increasingly powerful generative capabilities.

The advent of Generative AI (GenAI) represents a transformative leap: systems capable of synthesizing novel content-text, images, or audio-from learned data distributions. Unlike conventional AI, which focuses on classification or prediction, GenAI utilizes architectures such as Variational Autoencoders (VAEs), Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs), autoregressive transformer-based models, and diffusion models like Stable Diffusion, often trained on massive, minimally curated datasets. These architectural innovations have enabled unprecedented generative fidelity, and by 2025 models like GPT 4 and Gemini 2.0 are empowering enterprises to scale content creation, automate workflows, and personalize experiences. These systems have introduced novel privacy challenges-including emergent memorization and contextual inference-that transcend traditional governance frameworks [2].

 Technical Privacy Risks in Gen Ai

As Generative AI systems are deployed across consumer applications and enterprise environments, the complexity and severity of privacy risks have intensified. These risks are predominantly technical in nature, arising from the structural properties of Large Language Models (LLM) and their dependence on massive datasets, sophisticated architectures, and real-time inferencing. Unlike traditional rule-based systems, generative models are trained on heterogeneous, often unvetted corpora, and exhibit emergent behaviors that challenge established data governance norms. Such behaviors include hallucination, unauthorized memorization, and autonomous content generation, all of which pose risks to user privacy and data integrity.

The Stanford AI Index 2025 reports a 56.4% increase in AI-related incidents in 2024, totaling 233 reported casesmany of which involve privacy breaches such as deepfake generation, unauthorized data scraping, and inadvertent personal data exposure. While this trend demonstrates the accelerating privacy threat landscape, it also likely underestimates the true scale of the problem due to underreporting and reliance on media coverage (3). A Privacy and Security Concerns in Generative AI: A Comprehensive Survey identifies five intersecting lenses for evaluating GenAI risks - user, ethical, legal, technological, and institutional [5] yet technical analysis remains the most underexplored in current governance literature.

To contextualize the technical nature of these risks, it is essential to refer to structured security frameworks that specifically address vulnerabilities in large language models. The Open Worldwide Application Security Project (OWASP) - a globally recognized non-profit foundation established in 2001 and dedicated to improving software security through open, community-driven initiatives - develops free, consensus-based standards and methodologies used worldwide by developers, auditors, and regulators. Its projects aim to increase visibility into the most critical security issues across technologies and to promote best practices for mitigating them. Among its most widely adopted outputs is the "Top 10" series, which identifies the most prevalent and impactful categories of software vulnerabilities based on empirical data and expert analysis. In 2025, OWASP released the Top 10 for Large Language Model (LLM) Applications, a dedicated framework addressing security and privacy risks unique to generative AI systems [4]. This taxonomy was developed collaboratively by more than 500 contributors from academia, industry, and regulatory bodies, drawing from realworld incidents, red-team assessments, and empirical evidence.

The OWASP Top 10 for LLMs (2025 edition) offers a structured and technical taxonomy of such risks, drawing from community consensus and empirical incidents collected in 2024-2025. The taxonomy reflects risks such as prompt injection, insecure output handling, training data poisoning, model denial of service, plugin insecurities, and model theft. These risks do not exist in isolation; they are systemically embedded into the GenAI lifecycle, from model training and deployment to integration in external environments. Each threat reveals how privacy breaches can occur through vulnerabilities in prompt logic, data provenance, architectural permissions, or model behavior, with implications for both organizational compliance and end-user harm [4]. 2025 owasp top 10 list for llm and gen ai (4)

Risk Categorization and Mapping to Real World Examples

Privacy risks in generative AI systems manifest through specific technical vulnerabilities that are increasingly visible in real-world deployments. The OWASP 2025 Top 10 framework provides a valuable lens for identifying and analyzing these incidents. These risks are not isolated but interdependent, often cascading across system layers. The following categorization synthesizes real-world incidents aligned with OWASP 2025 classifications.

Data Exposure and Integrity Risks

Privacy risks from unintended data exposure and compromised integrity are prevalent in GenAI. LLM06: Excessive Agency addresses the risk of overly autonomous models performing unauthorized actions, potentially exposing sensitive data due to excessive permissions or functionality. LLM04: Data and Model Poisoning compounds this by enabling malicious data manipulation to corrupt outputs, risking private information disclosure.

The OpenAI ChatGPT macOS App Vulnerability (July 2024) illustrates this, where plain-text storage of user conversations risked sensitive data exposure, prompting an encryption update by OpenAI. This incident is listed in the National Vulnerability Database as CVE-2024-40594. Additional cases include the PoisonGPT experiment, in which manipulated web content was used to insert misinformation and backdoors into open-source models, demonstrating the real-world feasibility of data poisoning attacks [6]. Similarly, Hugging Face (2025) reported a security issue involving politically manipulated fine-tuned models that highlighted inadequate validation processes in hosted content [7].

 Manipulation and Explotation Risks

GenAI systems are susceptible to privacy breaches through manipulation or exploitation. LLM01: Prompt Injection allows attackers to manipulate inputs, disclosing sensitive data or generating unauthorized content. LLM10: Unbounded Consumption heightens this risk, where excessive or malicious queries can expose data via resource strain.

In December 2024, Italy's data protection authority (Garante) imposed a $€ 15$ million fine on OpenAI for GDPR violations after user prompts were found to inadvertently expose conversation data of other users [8]. Microsoft's Bing Copilot (2024) faced a series of incidents involving prompt injection attacks generating harmful or politically biased content [9]. Additionally, in 2025, a major European bank experienced recursive prompt loops in its GenAI-based customer service chatbot, resulting in the unintentional disclosure of sensitive client data and a temporary service outage [10].

 Operational and Systematic Risks

These risks stem from design flaws, third-party dependencies, and insufficient monitoring. LLM03: Supply Chain Vulnerabilities highlight the risk of untrusted datasets or plugins, potentially enabling data exfiltration or model corruption. LLM05: Improper Output Handling aggregates risk from unsanitized LLM outputs that lead to downstream security issues.

The Reddit vs. Anthropic case (June 2025) involves allegations of unauthorized data scraping used to train Claude AI, raising questions about supply chain due diligence [11]. The ChatGPT iOS plugin cache leak (January 2025) revealed cached plugin data containing sensitive content, illustrating improper output handling. Also in 2025, a compromised transformer uploaded to Hugging Face's repository disguised itself as a legitimate model and passed malware payloads to downstream systems [12].

The interconnectivity of these risks is evident. The OpenAI macOS vulnerability (LLM06) exposed consumer data and triggered regulatory scrutiny tied to exploitation (LLM01), illustrating how operational failures cascade across the AI lifecycle. The Anthropic case reflects systemic supply chain lapses (LLM03), emphasizing the need for more rigorous data provenance controls. Similarly, the OpenAI GDPR fine resulted from prompt exploitation (LLM10), underscoring the critical importance of secure input validation. Regulatory and Governance Response

The proliferation of GenAI technologies has outpaced the capacity of traditional regulatory mechanisms, revealing blind spots in data protection, accountability, and risk oversight. As illustrated by the complex and interconnected risks outlined in the previous section, organizations are grappling with vulnerabilities that span data leakage, prompt exploitation, supply chain fragility, and ungoverned model behaviors. These challenges cannot be mitigated by technical controls alone - they require governance structures that align legal obligations with operational and security frameworks.

A patchwork of international efforts is emerging in response. Different jurisdictions are adopting various approaches to AI regulation. In some regions, regulators have opted for a proactive stance, enacting comprehensive legislation designed to preempt potential risks [13].

In the European Union, the AI Act (Regulation (EU) 2024/1689), which came into force on 1 August 2024, marks the world's first binding horizontal regulation on AI. Full enforcement is expected by 2026. It introduces tiered risk classifications for AI systems and mandates conformity assessments, transparency disclosures, and governance mechanisms for general-purpose and high-risk AI systems. The General-Purpose AI Code of Practice, published by the EU AI Office in July 2025, serves as an interim instrument to prepare providers for compliance and includes commitments around transparency, data provenance, and safety auditing [18].

In the United States, federal AI governance has evolved through executive action. Executive Order 14110, issued in October 2023, had required federal agencies to appoint Chief AI Officers and implement the NIST AI RMF. However, in January 2025, Executive Order 13179 repealed these mandates, reflecting a strategic pivot toward innovation, interoperability, and voluntary risk management [15]. The resulting governance vacuum has been partially filled by sector-specific initiatives and voluntary adoption of standards.

Among the most widely adopted frameworks is the NIST AI Risk Management Framework (1.0), which remains foundational for U.S.-based organizations. As of 2025, NIST is collaborating with private sector stakeholders to refine implementation profiles for high-risk domains including healthcare, finance, and critical infrastructure [16]. Meanwhile, ISO/IEC 42001:2023 for Artificial Intelligence System Management (AISM) continues to gain traction in regulated industries, though no updated version has been released in 2025. Its certifiability has made it attractive in banking, insurance, and pharmaceuticals, particularly where synergies exist with established ISO/IEC standards.

Outside the EU and U.S., China's Interim Measures on Generative AI, in effect since August 2023, mandate algorithm registration, output watermarking, and security assessments. In early 2025, China's Cyberspace Administration advanced proposals for an expanded governance regime that emphasizes accountability, traceability, and alignment with state ideology [17]. Meanwhile, the Council of Europe is finalizing its Convention on Artificial Intelligence, Human Rights, Democracy and the Rule of Law, anticipated for adoption in late 2025. Canada's Artificial Intelligence and Data Act (AIDA) is undergoing revision to integrate GenAI-specific provisions.

In the UK, sector-specific regulatory guidance continues to evolve under the Department for Science, Innovation and Technology.

Despite these developments, several challenges remain. Many frameworks lack provisions for real-time oversight and output integrity assurance, particularly for generative models exhibiting autonomous behaviors. Jurisdictional fragmentation further complicates cross-border compliance, and voluntary standards, while flexible, require considerable institutional investment and technical expertise for effective deployment. As GenAI systems become more sophisticated, governance models must adapt to address runtime risks, emergent behavior, and global data flows. Conclusion

As generative AI continues to advance in capability and scope, the resulting privacy risks are evolving in both technical sophistication and systemic reach. This paper has explored how foundational vulnerabilities-categorized under the OWASP Top 10 for LLMs-are not merely theoretical risks but are already manifesting through realworld failures. From prompt injection and improper output handling to systemic agency overreach and data poisoning, GenAI privacy breaches are undermining user trust, exposing organizations to regulatory scrutiny, and posing novel governance challenges.

Although international regulatory frameworks, including the EU AI Act, NIST AI RMF, ISO/IEC 42001, and China's generative AI measures, signal progress in aligning governance with emerging AI risks, significant gaps persist. These include fragmented oversight, insufficient real-time enforcement mechanisms, and the resource-intensive nature of compliance with voluntary standards. The interconnectivity of technical vulnerabilities and regulatory fragmentation underscores the need for holistic, cross-functional risk management approaches that embed security and privacy-bydesign throughout the AI lifecycle.

Ultimately, mitigating technical privacy risks in generative AI will require a coordinated response across technical, organizational, and legal domains. Bridging the current gaps between governance expectations and technical implementations must become a priority for regulators, developers, and corporate leaders alike if generative AI is to evolve in a secure, trustworthy, and ethically responsible direction.

The rapid deployment of generative Artificial Intelligence (GenAI) systems has introduced a new class of technical privacy risks that exceed the scope of traditional data protection frameworks. This paper investigates these emerging vulnerabilities through the lens of the OWASP Top 10 for Large Language Models (LLMs) - 2025 edition, correlating them with real-world privacy failures from $2023-2025$. It categorizes incidents such as prompt injection, model poisoning, excessive agency, and improper output handling, drawing connections between architectural design choices and user-level data exposure. In parallel, the study evaluates international regulatory and governance responses, including the EU AI Act, NIST AI RMF, ISO/IEC 42001, and China's algorithmic controls, highlighting their strengths and implementation gaps. The analysis reveals that technical vulnerabilities often cascade across the GenAI lifecycle, creating enforcement blind spots and compliance challenges. The paper concludes by advocating for a holistic, cross-functional risk management strategy that integrates secure-by-design principles, real-time oversight, and proactive alignment with evolving regulatory standards.

The decision originates from an ex officio investigation initiated following the publication of press-reports regarding a data breach that occurred on March 2023. This landmark enforcement - complemented by a mandated six-month public awareness campaign - signals the growing confrontation between rapid AI innovation and privacy safeguards. It highlights pervasive vulnerabilities in how Generative Artificial Intelligence (GenAI) systems harvest and handle personal data.

While previous studies have focused primarily on ethical and legal implications by authors like Feuerriegel et al., 2024 [2] and Golda et al., 2024 [5], comparatively little attention has been given to the technical foundations of these risks - how specific system vulnerabilities enable privacy breaches in real-world applications. Recent research of Lyu et al., 2025 [19] has begun to systematize the understanding of such risks by mapping privacy attacks across the large language model (LLM) lifecycle, yet the connection between these technical vulnerabilities and regulatory or governance frameworks remains underexplored. Accordingly, this study examines the evolving landscape of technical privacy risks in generative AI by analyzing incidents reported between 2023 and 2025 and aligning them with established security taxonomies such as the Open Worldwide Application Security Project (OWASP) Top 10 for Large Language Models (LLMs). Microsoft's Bing Copilot (2024) faced a series of incidents involving prompt injection attacks generating harmful or politically biased content [9]. Additionally, in 2025, a major European bank experienced recursive prompt loops in its GenAI-based customer service chatbot, resulting in the unintentional disclosure of sensitive client data and a temporary service outage [10].

 Operational and Systematic Risks

These risks stem from design flaws, third-party dependencies, and insufficient monitoring. LLM03: Supply Chain Vulnerabilities highlight the risk of untrusted datasets or plugins, potentially enabling data exfiltration or model corruption. LLM05: Improper Output Handling aggregates risk from unsanitized LLM outputs that lead to downstream security issues.

The Reddit vs. Anthropic case (June 2025) involves allegations of unauthorized data scraping used to train Claude AI, raising questions about supply chain due diligence [11]. The ChatGPT iOS plugin cache leak (January 2025) revealed cached plugin data containing sensitive content, illustrating improper output handling. Also in 2025, a compromised transformer uploaded to Hugging Face's repository disguised itself as a legitimate model and passed malware payloads to downstream systems [12].

The interconnectivity of these risks is evident. The OpenAI macOS vulnerability (LLM06) exposed consumer data and triggered regulatory scrutiny tied to exploitation (LLM01), illustrating how operational failures cascade across the AI lifecycle. The Anthropic case reflects systemic supply chain lapses (LLM03), emphasizing the need for more rigorous data provenance controls. Similarly, the OpenAI GDPR fine resulted from prompt exploitation (LLM10), underscoring the critical importance of secure input validation. Regulatory and Governance Response

The proliferation of GenAI technologies has outpaced the capacity of traditional regulatory mechanisms, revealing blind spots in data protection, accountability, and risk oversight. As illustrated by the complex and interconnected risks outlined in the previous section, organizations are grappling with vulnerabilities that span data leakage, prompt exploitation, supply chain fragility, and ungoverned model behaviors. These challenges cannot be mitigated by technical controls alone - they require governance structures that align legal obligations with operational and security frameworks.

A patchwork of international efforts is emerging in response. Different jurisdictions are adopting various approaches to AI regulation. In some regions, regulators have opted for a proactive stance, enacting comprehensive legislation designed to preempt potential risks [13].

In the European Union, the AI Act (Regulation (EU) 2024/1689), which came into force on 1 August 2024, marks the world's first binding horizontal regulation on AI. Full enforcement is expected by 2026. It introduces tiered risk classifications for AI systems and mandates conformity assessments, transparency disclosures, and governance mechanisms for general-purpose and high-risk AI systems. The General-Purpose AI Code of Practice, published by the EU AI Office in July 2025, serves as an interim instrument to prepare providers for compliance and includes commitments around transparency, data provenance, and safety auditing [18].

In the United States, federal AI governance has evolved through executive action. Executive Order 14110, issued in October 2023, had required federal agencies to appoint Chief AI Officers and implement the NIST AI RMF. However, in January 2025, Executive Order 13179 repealed these mandates, reflecting a strategic pivot toward innovation, interoperability, and voluntary risk management [15]. The resulting governance vacuum has been partially filled by sector-specific initiatives and voluntary adoption of standards.

Among the most widely adopted frameworks is the NIST AI Risk Management Framework (1.0), which remains foundational for U.S.-based organizations. As of 2025, NIST is collaborating with private sector stakeholders to refine implementation profiles for high-risk domains including healthcare, finance, and critical infrastructure [16]. Meanwhile, ISO/IEC 42001:2023 for Artificial Intelligence System Management (AISM) continues to gain traction in regulated industries, though no updated version has been released in 2025. Its certifiability has made it attractive in banking, insurance, and pharmaceuticals, particularly where synergies exist with established ISO/IEC standards.

Outside the EU and U.S., China's Interim Measures on Generative AI, in effect since August 2023, mandate algorithm registration, output watermarking, and security assessments. In early 2025, China's Cyberspace Administration advanced proposals for an expanded governance regime that emphasizes accountability, traceability, and alignment with state ideology [17]. Meanwhile, the Council of Europe is finalizing its Convention on Artificial Intelligence, Human Rights, Democracy and the Rule of Law, anticipated for adoption in late 2025. Canada's Artificial Intelligence and Data Act (AIDA) is undergoing revision to integrate GenAI-specific provisions.

In the UK, sector-specific regulatory guidance continues to evolve under the Department for Science, Innovation and Technology.

Despite these developments, several challenges remain. Many frameworks lack provisions for real-time oversight and output integrity assurance, particularly for generative models exhibiting autonomous behaviors. Jurisdictional fragmentation further complicates cross-border compliance, and voluntary standards, while flexible, require considerable institutional investment and technical expertise for effective deployment. As GenAI systems become more sophisticated, governance models must adapt to address runtime risks, emergent behavior, and global data flows. Conclusion

As generative AI continues to advance in capability and scope, the resulting privacy risks are evolving in both technical sophistication and systemic reach. This paper has explored how foundational vulnerabilities-categorized under the OWASP Top 10 for LLMs-are not merely theoretical risks but are already manifesting through real-world failures. From prompt injection and improper output handling to systemic agency overreach and data poisoning, GenAI privacy breaches are undermining user trust, exposing organizations to regulatory scrutiny, and posing novel governance challenges.

Although international regulatory frameworks, including the EU AI Act, NIST AI RMF, ISO/IEC 42001, and China's generative AI measures, signal progress in aligning governance with emerging AI risks, significant gaps persist. These include fragmented oversight, insufficient real-time enforcement mechanisms, and the resource-intensive nature of compliance with voluntary standards. The interconnectivity of technical vulnerabilities and regulatory fragmentation underscores the need for holistic, cross-functional risk management approaches that embed security and privacy-by-design throughout the AI lifecycle.

Ultimately, mitigating technical privacy risks in generative AI will require a coordinated response across technical, organizational, and legal domains. Bridging the current gaps between governance expectations and technical implementations must become a priority for regulators, developers, and corporate leaders alike if generative AI is to evolve in a secure, trustworthy, and ethically responsible direction.
